description: Combined use greatly increases bleeding risk; aspirin impairs platelet aggregation and damages the gastric mucosa while warfarin suppresses clotting factor synthesis.
Ibuprofen inhibits the CYP2C9 enzyme that clears warfarin and also impairs platelet function; the combined effect substantially raises haemorrhage risk.
Naproxen displaces warfarin from protein-binding sites and inhibits platelet aggregation; gastrointestinal and systemic bleeding risk is significantly elevated.
Fluconazole potently inhibits CYP2C9; warfarin clearance falls and INR can increase dramatically; serious haemorrhage may occur.
Clarithromycin inhibits the enzymes that metabolise warfarin; INR rises and haemorrhage risk is substantially increased.
Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4-mediated warfarin clearance leading to elevated INR and increased bleeding risk.
Metronidazole markedly inhibits CYP2C9; warfarin plasma levels can double leading to serious bleeding risk.
Amiodarone has a very long half-life and persistently inhibits warfarin metabolism; INR may stay elevated for weeks or months after starting amiodarone.
Rifampicin potently induces CYP enzymes dramatically reducing warfarin plasma levels; patients may lose anticoagulant protection entirely.
Phenytoin initially inhibits then induces warfarin metabolism causing unpredictable INR fluctuations; very close monitoring is essential.
Diclofenac inhibits CYP2C9 and platelet function; the combination with warfarin substantially increases gastrointestinal and other bleeding risks.
Combining an anticoagulant with an antiplatelet agent greatly increases haemorrhage risk; use only when clinically justified with close monitoring.
Ciprofloxacin moderately inhibits warfarin metabolism; INR should be monitored after starting or stopping ciprofloxacin.
Omeprazole may modestly increase warfarin exposure; periodic INR monitoring is advisable during co-treatment.
Azithromycin may moderately increase warfarin levels; INR should be checked during co-administration.
St. John Wort potently induces CYP3A4 and CYP2C9; warfarin levels fall substantially increasing risk of thromboembolism.
Ketoconazole markedly inhibits warfarin metabolism; significant INR elevation and bleeding risk.
Calcium ions form insoluble chelate complexes with ciprofloxacin in the gut reducing its absorption by up to 50%.
Aluminium ions chelate fluoroquinolones in the gut; ciprofloxacin bioavailability can fall by up to 90% if taken simultaneously.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 raising theophylline plasma levels 2-3 fold; theophylline toxicity including seizures and cardiac arrhythmia may occur.
Calcium chelates levofloxacin in the gut reducing bioavailability; take antacids at least 2 hours apart from levofloxacin.
Penicillins compete with methotrexate for renal tubular secretion; methotrexate accumulates to toxic levels causing bone marrow suppression and mucositis.
Metronidazole inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase; alcohol is converted to toxic acetaldehyde causing severe flushing nausea vomiting and tachycardia.
Clarithromycin dramatically increases simvastatin plasma levels; risk of severe muscle damage including rhabdomyolysis is substantially elevated.
Clarithromycin raises atorvastatin exposure several-fold; rhabdomyolysis risk is substantially increased.
Rifampicin accelerates oestrogen and progestogen metabolism; contraceptive failure has been well-documented and alternative contraception is required.

mechanism: Renal tubular competition + reduced renal blood flow
description: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins impairing methotrexate excretion; methotrexate toxicity including bone marrow suppression can occur.

mechanism: Renal tubular competition + reduced renal blood flow
description: NSAIDs including naproxen impair methotrexate renal excretion; haematological toxicity is a serious risk.
Trimethoprim and methotrexate both inhibit dihydrofolate reductase; co-administration causes additive bone marrow suppression.
Co-trimoxazole contains trimethoprim and sulfamethoxazole both inhibiting folate metabolism; severe bone marrow toxicity can result when combined with methotrexate.
Concurrent SSRI and MAOI use causes serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia agitation clonus and potentially life-threatening cardiovascular instability.
SSRIs combined with MAOIs cause severe serotonin excess; this combination is absolutely contraindicated and life-threatening.
SSRIs and irreversible MAOIs must never be combined; at least 14 days must elapse after stopping the MAOI before starting an SSRI.
SNRIs including venlafaxine combined with MAOIs can cause life-threatening serotonin syndrome; absolutely contraindicated.
Paroxetine is a potent serotonin reuptake inhibitor; combined with an MAOI it causes life-threatening serotonin syndrome.
Tramadol inhibits serotonin reuptake; combined with an MAOI this causes serotonin syndrome and may also precipitate respiratory depression.
Fluoxetine inhibits tramadol metabolism via CYP2D6 and both increase synaptic serotonin; serotonin syndrome is a serious risk.
Sertraline blocks serotonin reuptake; tramadol also inhibits serotonin uptake; the combination risks serotonin syndrome with agitation and hyperthermia.
Dextromethorphan inhibits serotonin reuptake; combined with an MAOI it causes serotonin syndrome.
NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow; lithium renal excretion falls and plasma levels rise to toxic range causing tremor confusion and seizures.
Naproxen reduces renal prostaglandin synthesis impairing lithium excretion; lithium toxicity can develop rapidly.
Diclofenac raises lithium plasma levels by reducing renal clearance; lithium toxicity including coarse tremor and drowsiness may occur.
Thiazide diuretics cause sodium depletion which triggers renal lithium retention; lithium levels can rise to toxic concentrations.
Loop diuretics cause sodium and water loss which reduces lithium excretion; serum lithium levels can rise rapidly to toxic levels.
Tricyclic antidepressants increase lithium toxicity risk; the combination also raises serotonergic tone increasing neurotoxicity.
Theophylline increases renal lithium clearance potentially reducing lithium to sub-therapeutic levels; monitor lithium levels.
Haloperidol combined with lithium has been associated with irreversible neurotoxicity including encephalopathy even at normal serum lithium levels.
Amiodarone reduces digoxin clearance by about 50%; digoxin toxicity including nausea bradycardia and heart block can develop; dose reduction and monitoring required.
Verapamil doubles digoxin plasma levels and adds additive AV nodal depression; bradycardia and heart block are significant risks.
Diltiazem increases digoxin levels by 20-50%; cardiac monitoring and possible dose adjustment are needed.
Gemfibrozil inhibits simvastatin metabolism and hepatic uptake raising plasma levels many-fold; risk of severe rhabdomyolysis is greatly increased.
Gemfibrozil inhibits hepatic atorvastatin uptake; plasma levels rise substantially and myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk is elevated.
Gemfibrozil inhibits rosuvastatin hepatic clearance raising plasma levels; rhabdomyolysis is a serious risk.
Fenofibrate and simvastatin combination increases myopathy risk; less severe than with gemfibrozil but still requires monitoring.
Amlodipine modestly increases simvastatin exposure; simvastatin dose should not exceed 20 mg when combined with amlodipine.
Itraconazole dramatically elevates simvastatin plasma levels; rhabdomyolysis risk is very high and the combination should be avoided.
ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone causing potassium retention; additional potassium supplementation may cause dangerous hyperkalaemia and cardiac arrhythmia.
ACE inhibitors impair renal potassium excretion; supplemental potassium can push plasma levels to dangerous hyperkalaemic levels.
Combining an ACE inhibitor with potassium supplements risks severe hyperkalaemia particularly in patients with renal impairment.
Both ACE inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics reduce potassium excretion; the combination risks life-threatening hyperkalaemia.
ACE inhibitor plus aldosterone antagonist causes additive potassium retention; hyperkalaemia can cause cardiac arrest.
ACE inhibitor and aldosterone antagonist combination causes severe potassium retention; hyperkalaemia is a serious and potentially fatal risk.
Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 which converts clopidogrel to its active metabolite; platelet inhibition is reduced potentially increasing cardiovascular event risk.
Alcohol inhibits gluconeogenesis and increases lactate production; combined with metformin this raises the risk of lactic acidosis especially with binge drinking.
Alcohol suppresses hepatic glucose output and potentiates insulin action; serious prolonged hypoglycaemia can occur especially during fasting.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 reducing glipizide metabolism; hypoglycaemia can be severe and prolonged.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 raising sulfonylurea plasma levels; severe prolonged hypoglycaemia is a serious risk.
Both drugs cause profound nitric-oxide-mediated vasodilation; the combination can cause severe potentially fatal hypotension and is absolutely contraindicated.
Sildenafil potentiates nitrate-induced vasodilation causing severe hypotension; concurrent use with any nitrate is absolutely contraindicated.
Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase which breaks down azathioprine; azathioprine levels rise 3-4 fold causing severe bone marrow suppression.
Allopurinol blocks mercaptopurine metabolism; plasma levels increase dramatically causing life-threatening myelosuppression; dose reduction of 75% required.
Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 and accelerates hormonal contraceptive metabolism; contraceptive failure is well-documented.
Carbamazepine and lithium combination increases risk of neurotoxicity including cerebellar dysfunction even at normal plasma levels of both drugs.
Both drugs induce hepatic CYP enzymes and accelerate each other's metabolism; serum levels of both drugs may fall unexpectedly requiring dose adjustments.
Both drugs are independently hepatotoxic; co-administration during tuberculosis treatment substantially elevates the risk of drug-induced liver injury.
Alcohol is hepatotoxic and enhances isoniazid-induced hepatitis; patients should avoid alcohol during TB treatment.
Isoniazid inhibits phenytoin metabolism; phenytoin toxicity including ataxia nystagmus and confusion can develop and dose reduction may be required.
Both drugs prolong the cardiac QT interval; concurrent use risks torsades de pointes and life-threatening ventricular arrhythmia.
Clarithromycin prolongs the QT interval and inhibits amiodarone metabolism; severe arrhythmia risk including torsades de pointes.
Erythromycin inhibits terfenadine metabolism via CYP3A4 and prolongs QT interval; risk of fatal ventricular arrhythmia.
Both verapamil and beta-blockers depress AV conduction; co-administration risks severe bradycardia heart block and cardiac arrest.
Verapamil combined with metoprolol causes additive cardiac conduction slowing; potentially life-threatening bradycardia or heart block.
Diltiazem and beta-blockers in combination can cause excessive bradycardia and AV block; use with caution and ECG monitoring.
Both furosemide and aminoglycosides are ototoxic; concurrent use substantially increases the risk of irreversible sensorineural hearing loss.
Both drugs are nephrotoxic; the combination markedly increases the risk of acute kidney injury; renal function requires close monitoring.
Clarithromycin inhibits both colchicine transporters and CYP3A4 metabolism raising colchicine to toxic concentrations; colchicine toxicity can be fatal.
Cyclosporine inhibits colchicine efflux transporters causing colchicine accumulation; severe neuromuscular toxicity and multi-organ failure can occur.
Verapamil inhibits colchicine efflux; colchicine accumulates to potentially toxic levels; neuromuscular toxicity has been reported.
Fluconazole raises cyclosporine plasma levels substantially; nephrotoxicity and other cyclosporine toxicities are significantly increased.
Fluconazole markedly increases tacrolimus blood levels; nephrotoxicity and immunosuppression toxicity risk is substantially elevated.
MAOIs combined with levodopa cause severe hypertensive crisis and hyperpyrexia due to excess catecholamine release.
Linezolid is a weak MAOI; tramadol also increases synaptic serotonin; the combination risks serotonin syndrome with agitation hyperthermia and clonus.
Linezolid inhibits MAO and fluoxetine blocks serotonin reuptake; life-threatening serotonin syndrome can occur; avoid the combination.
Linezolid combined with SSRIs creates excess synaptic serotonin; serotonin syndrome is a serious and potentially fatal risk.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6 which converts tamoxifen to active endoxifen; reduced endoxifen levels may compromise breast cancer treatment efficacy.
Paroxetine is one of the most potent CYP2D6 inhibitors; co-administration markedly reduces active tamoxifen metabolite levels and anticancer efficacy.
Erythromycin inhibits theophylline metabolism raising plasma levels; theophylline toxicity including nausea palpitation and seizures can occur.
Chronic heavy alcohol use induces CYP2E1 which converts paracetamol to the hepatotoxic metabolite NAPQI; even therapeutic doses can cause liver damage.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP3A4 increasing alprazolam plasma levels; excessive sedation and respiratory depression may occur.
Proton pump inhibitors reduce renal tubular secretion of methotrexate; methotrexate levels may rise and toxicity including mucositis and myelosuppression can result.
Valproate inhibits carbamazepine metabolism while carbamazepine induces valproate clearance; unpredictable levels of both drugs require careful monitoring.
See Valproate-Carbamazepine interaction — bidirectional enzyme induction and inhibition makes both drug levels unpredictable.
